summaries.history: The Giovanni began as a mortal merchant family in ancient Rome, practicing ancestor worship and death magic (nigrimancy). In 1005, Augustus Giovanni was Embraced by the Cappadocian Antediluvian, who sought to learn the Giovanni's secrets of communicating with the dead. Augustus and his family had other plans: they learned from the Cappadocians, grew powerful, and in 1444, Augustus diablerized the Cappadocian founder, elevating himself to Third Generation and destroying the Cappadocian clan. The Giovanni then systematically eliminated the remaining Cappadocians, with the Camarilla's tacit approval in the Promise of 1528, which isolated the Giovanni from Kindred politics in exchange for ending the 'clan war.' Since then, the Giovanni have built a global financial empire, expanded into multiple family branches (Dunsirn, Pisanob, Milliners, and minor families), and pursued their ultimate goal: the Endless Night, a ritual that will tear down the barrier between the living and the dead.
summaries.culture: Giovanni culture is built on family, wealth, and necromantic power. They are a true family business, with intense loyalty mixed with suffocating control. Family members are expected to serve the clan's interests above their own. The clan values 'single-blooded' childer—those born from incestuous unions within the family—believing them superior to 'double-blooded' outsiders. This has led to a culture of sexual deviancy and family dysfunction that outsiders find repulsive. The Giovanni practice the Proxy Kiss, ghouling family members before the Embrace to ensure loyalty and test worthiness. They organize by family branches, with the main Giovanni line in Venice, the Dunsirn in Scotland, the Pisanob in Mexico, the Milliners in America, and various minor families worldwide.
summaries.philosophy: Giovanni philosophy centers on wealth, power, and family legacy. They believe that money is the ultimate tool—it buys influence, protection, and silence. They see death as a resource to be managed, and the dead as sources of information and power. The clan's ultimate goal is the Endless Night, when the barrier between the living and the dead will be torn down, giving the Giovanni ultimate power. They justify their actions—incest, murder, necromancy, and worse—as 'for the family.' They operate outside Camarilla and Sabbat politics, believing that isolation protects them while they prepare for Gehenna.
summaries.factions: The Giovanni are organized into family branches: the main Giovanni line in Venice (led by Augustus and his direct descendants), the Dunsirn in Scotland (merchants and traders), the Pisanob in Mexico (practicing Aztec-influenced necromancy), the Milliners in America (newer, less respected), and various minor families worldwide. Within the main family, there are the anziani (elders) who make decisions, divided into anziani prudente (cautious elders) and anziani appassionati (eager elders). There are also the Premascines—Giovanni Embraced before Augustus diablerized the Cappadocian founder—who may still exist in hiding. The clan's ultimate leadership rests with Augustus Giovanni, though day-to-day operations are handled by his most trusted descendants.
summaries.politics: The Giovanni operate outside both the Camarilla and Sabbat, bound by the Promise of 1528 to remain apolitical. This isolation protects them from sect conflicts but also means they have no allies when trouble comes. They use their wealth and necromantic knowledge to influence events without direct involvement. Other Kindred resent them for their isolation, their wealth, and their role in destroying the Cappadocians. The Giovanni don't care—they're playing a longer game, preparing for the Endless Night while other Kindred fight over territory and power.
summaries.relations: The Giovanni have strained relationships with most other clans. The Camarilla resents their isolation and wealth. The Sabbat views them as traitors who destroyed an entire clan. The Tremere fear their necromantic power. The Ventrue compete with them for financial influence. The Toreador resent their patronage of the arts. Most clans simply avoid them, viewing them as dangerous, amoral, and untrustworthy. The Giovanni, for their part, see other Kindred as temporary obstacles or potential assets—nothing more.
summaries.character_creation: Giovanni characters should emphasize Mental Attributes (Intelligence, Wits, Perception) and Social Attributes (Manipulation, Charisma). Physical Attributes are less important unless the character is a Dunsirn enforcer. Favored Disciplines are Dominate, Necromancy, and Potence. Backgrounds often include Resources (the clan is wealthy), Status (within the family), Generation (many are low generation due to Augustus's power), and Retainers (ghouls and wraiths). The clan weakness—their Kiss causes excruciating pain—should be considered when building characters, as it makes feeding more difficult and memorable.
summaries.archetypes: Common archetypes include: the Business Magnate who runs corporate empires; the Necromancer who specializes in the Black Art; the Family Enforcer who protects family interests through violence; the Financial Advisor who manages the clan's wealth; the Premascine who remembers the old ways; the Dunsirn Merchant who trades in goods and information; the Pisanob Ritualist who practices Aztec-influenced necromancy; the Milliner Outsider who struggles for respect; the Family Spy who gathers information through wraiths; and the Degenerate who embraces the clan's darker practices.
summaries.storyteller_advice: Use Giovanni to inject wealth, necromantic knowledge, and moral compromise into your chronicle. Their isolation from Kindred politics makes them wild cards—they can provide information, resources, or services that other clans cannot, but always for a price. The clan's family structure creates opportunities for loyalty conflicts, generational disputes, and the tension between personal ambition and family duty. Their necromantic abilities make them valuable sources of information but also dangerous enemies. Use them to show the cost of power and wealth, and the moral compromises necessary to maintain both.

tone_highlights.emotional_temperature: Cold, calculating, and opulent. The Giovanni don't hide their nature—they make their victims suffer for it.
tone_highlights.social_posture: Wealthy, cultured, and morally flexible. They justify everything as 'for the family' or 'it's business.'
tone_highlights.predator_style: They feed with excruciating pain, making their victims remember every moment. They use wealth and necromancy to control situations rather than direct violence.
recurring_motifs: The Endless Night as ultimate goal

common_archetypes.name: Business Magnate
common_archetypes.description: A Giovanni who runs corporate empires, using wealth to control mortals and influence events.

common_archetypes.name: Necromancer
common_archetypes.description: A Giovanni who specializes in the Black Art, consulting with the dead and commanding spirits.

common_archetypes.name: Family Enforcer
common_archetypes.description: A Giovanni who protects family interests through violence, intimidation, and elimination of threats.

common_archetypes.name: Dunsirn Merchant
common_archetypes.description: A Giovanni from the Scottish Dunsirn branch, trading in goods, information, and favors.
